item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is owned by Lulu Hoeller and is released under CC BY 2.0. As such, it is compliant with the site's license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting Related Trivia: António Egas Moniz won the 1949 Nobel Prize in medicine for inventing the prefrontal lobotomy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This went from "meh" to "GAH" really fast. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so… its a ghost that does lobotomies?
unless im missing something this is boring.
no vote though, cause it was well written and the picture was good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture choice is fantastic. There isn't really anything else here, though. It's bog-standard in every way. -1'd, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because lobotomies are just plain horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
@Litfried: One major difference between taking antidepressants and practicing a lobotomy is that you can switch from an antidepressant to another, and there is
pharmaceutical research on the topic that might eventually lay better results with a new drug.

However, there is little or no progress in the field of regrowing brain after a lobotomy. And, frankly, lobotomies back then where relatively crude. Neurosurgery is still done this days, although it is far more precise and it is done only in cases where medical treatment doesn't give the patient even a far shot of having a pleasant, let alone normal, life. I would still prefer antidepressants, though.

Also, no vote. This page screamed lobotomy the moment I started reading it, even if it is rather well written, and I coincide with many other users in their respective points: it is a haunted item, there is no need for the chair, there is no particular surprise or twist… while the idea of ghosts being creepy because that's how the people they were were creepy is always nice.

item.title: Re: Signatures
item.content:encoded:
and here's another point.

The SCP does a routine physical on the patient. What happens if it finds something physically wrong, like (for instance) cancer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a pretty standard haunted item. I guessed what it would do right from the get-go. That's not a complaint, though. I thoroughly enjoyed this. Not every SCP has to be super-scary and really complex. Also, it got me thinking about the horrors of lobotomy, and I managed to scare myself by thinking 'at least the lobotomee doesn't have to suffer horrendous depression any more. Is it so different from modern drugs that kill your personality along with the depression, just more severe?' Thought-provoking is a big tick in my book!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed the "Item Number" in "Item #". Revert it if you think I am wrong.

item.title: Signatures
item.content:encoded: if you activate the SCP and you have a mild psychiatric illness, the apparition gives you a prescription. Aren't all prescriptions signed? The Foundation should love having that data.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd rather have a bottle in front of me than a ghost doing a frontal lobotomy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people just love their jobs so much that they don't ever want to stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Of note is that the entity has an uncanny ability to determine"
seems out of tone. Assuming he has an ability, and inparticular describing said ability as "uncanny", doesn't quite fit. You might want to reword that part of the sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Psychiatrists are medical doctors and have a licence to perform physicals, etc. Modern psychs have decided that they just want to prescribe medications and so don't even touch patients anymore. That's not the way it used to be.

I like how this takes the time to diagnose patients and treats them to the best of its knowledge. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honorary +1 for your comment due to "LOBOTOMY TIME!".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to echo Scantron's sentiment- I just don't find this interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it "Item Number" instead of "Item #"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who suffers from several psychiatric disorders, this struck a chord with me. The fact that it doesn't just go "LOBOTOMY TIME!" the second anyone sits down and instead actually goes through the whole therapist thing was a nice change from the lobotomy mad doctors of other horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that the situation ramps up depending on what the subject's ailments are. Also slightly creeped out that an apparent physician (who does actually perform physical examinations) is also giving psychiatric evaluations. Or am I misinterpreting his position? Anyway, it's not just a chair that lobotomizes you, and it's good to see that picture coming back on an article that works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It certainly is a thing that's there, and that's one thing I like about it. I was entirely prepared for it to compel people to take a seat.
And on the point of the picture, I'd have to say I agree. It looks way too ermagerdspooky and staged- it doesn't help with suspension of disbelief, which is generally the point of images (in my opinion).

No vote for me. The article has some merits, but it feels like it's missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. This makes good use of the picture in my opinion.
One thing I noticed is that in the first paragraph of the description it says

As long as the subject does not sit in SCP-1892 they are under no compulsion to follow SCP-1892-1’s directions and may leave the area freely.

And never really mentions the imaginary man giving orders which must be followed again. Also maybe throw in testing of some kind. That'd sell this really well for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it needs some more material. It's a chair that lobotomizes and is pretty boring otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. I'm not sure what I'm supposed to get out of this, really. It's just sort of a thing that's there, and this happens if you use it.

As an aside, I think this could lose the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly like this, but there were a few stylistic issues that bugged me. First off, I find the very last sentence unwieldy, mainly because "that of" lacks a clear referent (if it refers to "lesions", it should be "those of"). And then there's this:

Subjects diagnosed with severe psychiatric illness (major depression, auditory/visual hallucinations, etc.) report SCP-1892-1 muttering to itself before sighing and stating a lack of alternatives. SCP-1892-1 will state its intent to restrain the subject, stating its desire to avoid inadvertent harm to the subject.

That's quite a lot of statements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang, I like this. One thing tho, it might just be me and the fact that I don't see old stuff like this very often, but the description and the picture seem… off, to me. It really might just be me, though, given that I haven't had much exposure to such things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yes. I know that fact very well. But it's something like "I've seen it played for horror too many times" and therefore I'm not too fascinated by "psychiatry was spooky in the past! Dem scary lobotomies" anymore. Just old and cynical I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, and thanks for the input. I focused on the lobotomies because that actually WAS how they treated some patients with severe psychiatric illness for a short (but disturbing) amount of time. Transorbital lobotomies are the stuff of f**king nightmares (link: http://www.npr.org/programs/atc/features/2005/nov/lobotomy/icepick.html)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2spooky4me ghostlobotomies.

I wish I could like it more. I was liking it up until ghostlobotomies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the upvote. Any chance you could narrow down what you mean? I thought about expanding on some things a bit, but wanted to keep it short and focused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like a bit more could have been done with this, but well done regardless, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

+1

Edit: Looking at it again, you have a 1 before your footnote and I'm not sure outstripping is good tone. Maybe surpassing. Otherwise still good.
